God assures his people that he will again enter into a covenant relationship with himself. When facing difficult times and things look bad, it is good to remember that the church has experienced this before. But it can be hard to find comfort in past blessings when you are currently experiencing hardship; yet it is a source of happiness for those who, through grace, share in God’s love—a love that lasts forever, from everlasting plans to everlasting continuation. Those whom God loves with this kind of love will be drawn to himself by the influence of his Spirit on their souls. When we praise God for what he has done, we must also ask him for the blessings his church needs and expects. When the Lord calls, we should not say that we cannot come; because he who calls us will help us and strengthen us. God’s goodness will lead them to repentance. And they will weep over sin with more bitterness and tenderness when delivered from their captivity than while suffering under it. If we consider God our Father and join the church of the firstborn, we will lack nothing that is good for us. These predictions likely refer also to a future gathering of the Israelites from all parts of the world. And they figuratively describe the conversion of sinners to Christ and the clear and safe path by which they are led.

He who scattered Israel knows where to find them. It is comforting to observe God’s goodness in his provision. But our souls are never as valuable as gardens unless they are watered with the dews of God's Spirit and grace. A precious promise follows, which will not be fully realized except in the heavenly Zion. Let them be certain of God’s loving-kindness, and they will be satisfied with it and desire nothing more to make them happy. Rachel is represented as rising from her grave and refusing comfort, believing that her offspring have been destroyed. The murder of the children at Bethlehem by Herod fulfilled this prediction to some degree, but could not represent its full meaning. If we have hope for an eternal inheritance, both for ourselves and those belonging to us, all temporal afflictions can be endured and will work out for our good.

Ephraim (the ten tribes) is weeping over sin. He is angry with himself for his sin, folly, and stubbornness. He realizes that he cannot, by his own power, remain close to God, much less return when he has strayed. Therefore, he prays, ‘Turn me back, and I will turn.’ His will was submitted to the will of God. When the teaching of God’s Spirit accompanied the corrections of his providence, then the work was done. This is our comfort in affliction: that the Lord remembers us. God has mercy in store—rich mercy, sure mercy, suitable mercy—for all who seek him sincerely.

The way from the bondage of sin to the freedom of God’s children is a highway—plain and safe; yet few are likely to walk on it unless they set their hearts toward it. They are encouraged by the promise of something new and extraordinary: a creation, a work of Almighty power—the human nature of Christ, formed and prepared by the power of the Holy Spirit. This is mentioned as an encouragement for the Jews to return to their own land. And they are given a comforting prospect of a happy settlement there. God has joined godliness and honesty; no one should try to separate them or think that one can compensate for the lack of the other. In God’s love and favor, the weary soul will find rest, and the sorrowful will find joy. And what could we see with more satisfaction than the good of Jerusalem and peace upon Israel?

The people of God will become numerous and prosperous. This place is quoted as the summary of the covenant of grace made with believers in Jesus Christ. It is not, ‘I will give them a new law,’ for Christ did not come to destroy the law but to fulfill it; rather, the law will be written on their hearts by the finger of the Spirit, just as it was formerly written on stone tablets. The Lord will, by his grace, make his people willing in the day of his power. All will know the Lord; all will be welcome to the knowledge of God and will have the means for that knowledge. There will be an outpouring of the Holy Spirit at the time the gospel is proclaimed. No one will finally perish because of their own sins—none who are willing to accept Christ’s salvation.

Just as surely as the heavenly bodies will continue on their established course, according to the will of their Creator, until the end of time, and just as the sea obeys him, so surely will the Jews be continued as a separate people. Words can scarcely express more strongly the restoration of Israel. The rebuilding of Jerusalem, its enlargement, and its establishment will serve as an assurance of the great things God will do for the gospel church. The personal happiness of every true believer, along with the future restoration of Israel, is secured by promise, covenant, and oath. This divine love surpasses understanding; and to those who embrace it, every present blessing is a guarantee of salvation.
